answer:
**Vīramitrodaya, Chapter 1** (also known as *Samayaprakāśa*, "The Illumination of Time," the first section of Mitra Miśra's *Vīramitrodaya*, a 17th-century dharmaśāstra digest).[^1]

The introductory verse (verse 7) on Medinīmalla reads:

> There was, in the lineage of the Sīkāśīrāja, a universal sovereign over all the earth, an ornament to the circle of kings, like the moon in the ocean: the glorious Medinīmalla by name, **a wrestler upon the earth with the weight of his mighty forces**. He resembled the mighty Bhīma, gladdening the world by crushing the wicked and fulfilling the desires of supplicants, **his fame as white as the brilliance of the serpent king Ādiśeṣa**. // 7 //

The genealogy continues: from Medinīmalla arises Arjuna (verses 9–10); from Arjuna, Malakhāna (verses 11–12); from Malakhāna, Pratāparudra (verse 13).[^2]

[^1]: Mitra Miśra, *Vīramitrodaya*, Chapter 1, [translation](/works/unknown-viramitrodaya-d4b632/01/translation/).

[^2]: Mitra Miśra, *Vīramitrodaya*, Chapter 1, [paragraphs](/works/unknown-viramitrodaya-d4b632/01/translation/#p-a96338) p-a96338 (Arjuna); [p-602e62](/works/unknown-viramitrodaya-d4b632/01/translation/#p-602e62) (Malakhāna); [p-bbf6fa](/works/unknown-viramitrodaya-d4b632/01/translation/#p-bbf6fa) (Pratāparudra).
